Were You Asked This?
Design a system to display aggregation of events where multiple robots are continuously generating events in realtime

Design a real-time event aggregation system that ingests continuous data streams from multiple robots and provides a dashboard for monitoring and querying with 1-minute granularity.

Asked at:

U

UiPath

H

Hopper

Google

A real-time robot event aggregator is a streaming analytics platform built to ingest continuous telemetry from fleets of robots and surface minute-level metrics on a live dashboard. Think of it like the analytics back-end behind IoT fleets, industrial automation, or ad-click counters—events flow in continuously, get aggregated per minute, and operators monitor health and trends in near real time.

Interviewers ask this to probe your streaming-data fundamentals: event-time vs processing-time semantics, windowed aggregations, handling out-of-order data, exactly-once/idem-potent processing, and the trade-offs between freshness and correctness. They also look for your ability to scale hot write paths, design queryable time-series stores, and push efficient real-time updates to dashboards while keeping costs and operational complexity in check.

Community Solutions
Design a system to display aggregation of events where multiple robots are continuously generating events in realtime
by SmallHarlequinMarsupial210
3
9
Common Functional Requirements

Most candidates end up covering this set of core functionalities

Users should be able to monitor live fleet-wide and per-robot metrics aggregated into 1-minute buckets with near real-time latency.

Users should be able to query historical aggregates over a time range with filters (e.g., robot, group, event type) at 1-minute granularity.

Users should be able to receive automatic dashboard updates as new minute buckets close or are corrected by late-arriving events.

Users should be able to configure alerts on aggregated metrics (e.g., thresholds, rate anomalies) and get notified promptly.

Common Deep Dives

Common follow-up questions interviewers like to ask for this question

How will you handle out-of-order and late-arriving events while maintaining accurate 1-minute aggregates and acceptable freshness?

How will you scale ingestion and aggregation to millions of events per minute without hotspots or bottlenecks?

How will you ensure idempotency and exactly-once (or effectively-once) aggregation in the presence of retries and duplicates?

How will you serve low-latency queries and push timely updates to dashboards at scale?

Relevant Patterns

Relevant patterns that you should know for this question

Scaling Writes

Robot fleets generate continuous high-throughput event streams. You must partition ingestion, pre-aggregate locally, and avoid hot keys so that minute-level windows close on time under heavy write load.

Read More
Real-time Updates

Operators expect the dashboard to reflect new minute buckets and late corrections immediately. Pushing updates via WebSockets/SSE and managing provisional vs finalized state is central to the user experience.

Read More
Dealing with Contention

Many events update the same minute bucket concurrently (e.g., fleet-wide counters). Without careful sharding, combiners, and idempotent upserts, you’ll see hotspots, lock contention, and inconsistent counts.

Read More
Relevant Technologies

Relevant technologies that could be used to solve this question

Kafka

Kafka reliably buffers high-throughput robot telemetry with backpressure and retention for reprocessing. Partitioning by robotId/eventType spreads load, and idempotent producers plus consumer groups support exactly-once/effectively-once pipelines.

Read More
Flink

Flink provides event-time windowing, watermarks, allowed lateness, and exactly-once sinks—ideal for 1-minute tumbling window aggregations and late data corrections in a streaming analytics pipeline.

Read More
Cassandra

Cassandra’s wide-row, time-series friendly schema and linear scalability make it a strong fit for storing per-minute aggregates keyed by time and dimensions, supporting high write throughput and low-latency reads.

Read More
Similar Problems to Practice

Related problems to practice for this question

Ad Click Aggregator

Both systems ingest large, continuous event streams and compute per-minute (or per-time-window) aggregates, requiring event-time processing, deduplication, and scalable write paths.

Read
Practice
FB Live Comments

Real-time fan-out and low-latency updates to many clients mirrors the dashboard push model here. Both benefit from pub/sub, throttling, and incremental updates for active views.

Read
Practice
Rate Limiter

Windowed counters and contention on hot keys are shared concerns. Techniques like sharded counters and time-bucketed keys apply directly to 1-minute aggregate updates.

Read
Practice
Red Flags to Avoid

Common mistakes that can sink candidates in an interview

Counting by processing time and ignoring event time/watermarks

This produces incorrect minute buckets when events arrive late or out of order, leading to flapping dashboards and mistrust in metrics.

Computing aggregates on read by scanning raw events

On-demand scans won’t meet latency at scale and will explode costs. You need precomputed, windowed aggregates to deliver consistent sub-second queries.

Single global aggregator or hotspot key for minute buckets

A central reducer or time-based partition key creates contention and drops throughput under peak load, risking backlogs, stale data, and missed SLAs.

Question Timeline

See when this question was last asked and where, including any notes left by other candidates.

Late February, 2026

H

Hopper

Manager

This system design challenge focuses on building a Big Data pipeline that transforms billions of raw search logs into a high-performance "Price Trend" feature. At its core, the problem is a Stream Processing task: we must ingest real-time search events (Hotel ID, dates, and price) through a distributed queue like Kafka, process them using a framework like Apache Flink to calculate moving averages and price percentiles, and then store those pre-computed "color maps" in a low-latency NoSQL database like DynamoDB. Because we are serving 50 million users, we cannot calculate these trends on the fly; instead, we use a Lambda Architecture where a "Fast Layer" handles today's price spikes and a "Batch Layer" (using Spark or Snowflake) periodically recalibrates the historical "Green/Yellow/Red" thresholds. By storing the entire 365-day calendar as a single compressed object, we ensure that when a user opens the Hopper app, the calendar loads in under 100ms, providing a seamless and data-driven booking experience.

Early November, 2025

Google

Staff

Design a Time Reordering Queue System

The system receives events from multiple independent sources.

Each event contains a timestamp.

The system should expose an API that returns events ordered by timestamp in descending order.

It must be capable of handling up to 1 million events per second.

Consider how to handle out-of-order (late) or boundary events efficiently.

The interviewer expects discussion around file-based storage, scalability, and system design principles — including data flow, parallel processing, configuration management, and reasoning behind key configuration choices.

Early July, 2025

U

UiPath

Mid-level
